text: We are introducing a three-chakra tubeless noses technology in rectocephalic cancer. It is a sixty-two-year-old male with a high rectal lesion showing in colonoscopy. Pathology confirmed that it is an adenocarcinoma. CT scan shows this is a localized disease in the high rectum without distant metastasis.
During the abdominal cavity exploration, the surgeon observes no metastasis in the liver or omentum. The primary tumor is visually identified in the high rectum.

The surgeon begins by moving to the medial area to dissect lymph nodes and soft tissues along the inferior mesenteric artery. The dissection follows the plane between the fascia and mesocolon, expanding the plane to free the sigmoid colon. The left ureter is identified and preserved during this process.

The surgeon skeletonizes the distal rectum and closes the rectal lumen at the distal margin using ligation. The distal rectum is then washed with Adover's solution from the anus. The proximal margins of the resection are confirmed.
The surgeon closes the proximal colon lumen by ligation following skeletonization. After multiple washes, the distal rectal lumen appears clean. The bowel is then transected using a harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon separates the bowel wall using a sterilized film and then places the stapler anvil into the abdominal cavity through the rectal lumen from the anus.

The surgeon amputates the specimen from the proximal colon and extracts it through the anus. The distal lumen is closed using an Ethicon EndoGIA stapler, followed by multiple washes to minimize contamination. The staple anvil is then placed into the proximal lumen to complete the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs bubble testing on the anastomosis, confirming no air leakage. Subsequently, the mesocolon hiatus is closed using surgical techniques.
The surgeon closes the chakra incisions, completing the procedure. The surgical field is clear, with no tubes remaining on the patient post-surgery.

The duration of the surgery is about nineteen minutes and the blood loss is less than fifty milliliters. The patient can take some liquid right after the surgery and walk around the following day. Bowel movement recovers at the second day and patient is discharged at the fifth day after surgery without complications. Thanks for your attention.
